disease.name: Varicose Veins
disease.note: Dilated, tortuous superficial veins of the lower limb due to venous valve incompetence. CEAP classification guides management. Common in East Africa among occupations requiring prolonged standing — farming, market vending, teaching, and security work. Multi-parity, obesity with urbanization, and limited access to compression hosiery contribute to advanced presentation with skin changes and venous ulceration. Venous ulcers cause significant disability and are often wrongly attributed to 'worms' or 'poisoned blood' by traditional healers, delaying proper wound care. Endovenous and surgical options are available in major Kenyan referral centres but access remains limited in rural areas. CEAP classification standardizes assessment.
presentingComplaints: Visible swollen veins in the leg — like ropes or cords
Legs feel heavy, tired, or aching at the end of the day
Ankle swelling that goes down overnight
Pain along the course of the veins
Itching or burning over the veins — especially lower shin
Darkening of the skin around the ankle
Open sore or wound on the lower leg that will not heal
Previous ulcer that has healed but left a scar
Bleeding from a vein that burst
Red, painful, hard lump along a vein

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Active bleeding/ulcer with infection/phlegmasia/thrombophlebitis ascending -> SEVERE; C1-C4 disease without complications -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: When did you first notice the enlarged veins in your leg?
Do you have any pain, heaviness, or aching in your legs?
Is the pain worse at the end of the day or after prolonged standing?
Does the pain improve when you lie down and elevate your legs?
Do your ankles swell, especially in the evening?
Do the veins look more prominent during pregnancy or after long periods on your feet?
Have you noticed any skin changes — darkening, thickening, or itching around the ankle?
Have you ever had a leg ulcer (open sore) on your lower leg?
How long did the ulcer take to heal? Has it come back?
Have you had any bleeding from the varicose veins — even a small amount?
Have you had a red, painful, hard cord along a vein (thrombophlebitis)?
Do you have a family history of varicose veins?
How many pregnancies and deliveries have you had?
What work do you do — do you stand for long hours?
Have you ever had a DVT (blood clot) or pulmonary embolism?
Have you had any leg injury, fracture, or surgery in the past?
Do you wear compression stockings? Have you ever tried them?
Have you seen a traditional healer or used herbal remedies for your legs?
Do you smoke or use khat (miraa)?
Do you have diabetes or high blood pressure?
Are the veins in one leg or both legs?
Do you have difficulty walking or doing daily work because of your legs?
Have you had any previous treatment for varicose veins — injections, laser, or surgery?
Do you have any chronic cough or constipation (increases intra-abdominal pressure)?
Do you do heavy lifting at work or at home?
Have you ever had an ultrasound of your leg veins before?
Do you have any known pelvic varicose veins or pelvic congestion syndrome?
Have you ever had pelvic surgery (hysterectomy, C-section, hernia repair)?
Do you have heavy periods or fibroids (estrogen influence)?
Have you ever had treatment for infertility or had multiple IVF cycles?
Do you take any hormonal medications (contraceptive pill, HRT, tamoxifen)?
Have you noticed your veins getting worse with each pregnancy?
Do you use traditional herbal remedies for your leg veins?
Have you had any blood clots in your family?
Do your legs feel better or worse in hot weather?
Do the veins bother you more for cosmetic reasons or symptoms?
Have you ever tried compression stockings and did they help?
Do you have any difficulty finding shoes that fit due to leg swelling?
How many hours per day do you spend standing vs sitting vs walking?
Did the veins appear suddenly or gradually over years?
Have you noticed any changes in the color of your skin around the ankle?
Have you ever had an episode where your leg became suddenly very swollen, red, and painful?

examination_findings.finding: Visible tortuous, dilated superficial veins in the lower limb — along saphenous system or perforators
Ankle edema — pitting, worse at end of day, improves with elevation
Hemosiderin pigmentation — brown discoloration in the gaiter area (lower third of leg)
Lipodermatosclerosis — fibrotic, indurated, woody skin in gaiter area
Active venous ulcer — shallow, irregular borders, in gaiter area, with granulation or slough base
Healed venous ulcer scar — atrophic, white or pigmented scar in gaiter area
Stasis dermatitis — erythema, scaling, itching around the ankle
Atrophie blanche — white, atrophic, porcelain-like patches with telangiectasias
Corona phlebectatica (venous flare) — fan-shaped intradermal telangiectasias at medial foot or ankle
Palpable varicose veins — feel as soft, compressible, dilated tubes beneath the skin
Positive Trendelenburg test — rapid filling on release of tourniquet indicates SFJ incompetence
Positive Perthes test — increased distension on calf contraction suggests deep vein obstruction
Cough impulse at saphenofemoral junction — palpable thrill on coughing
Tap test — fluid thrill transmitted along a varicose vein on percussion
Difference in leg circumference (calf/thigh) >3cm compared to contralateral
Palpable venous ulcer base depth and assess for exposed tendon or bone (suspected osteomyelitis)
Assess for pelvic varicose veins — vulvar, perineal, or suprapubic varicosities suggesting pelvic congestion syndrome
Brodie-Trendelenburg test (with tourniquet at different levels) — localizes the level of incompetent perforators
Pratt's test (two-bandage method) — sequential release to identify incompetent perforator segments
Fegan's method — direct finger pressure over suspected perforator sites while squeezing the calf
Measure ankle circumference (at narrowest point above malleoli) and calf circumference (maximal point) bilateral
Feel the temperature of the leg with the dorsum of your hand — warmth suggests inflammation or DVT
Skin dryness, scaling, or fissuring in the gaiter area (stasis dermatitis)
Eczema or allergic contact dermatitis (often from topical treatments or dressings)
Assess ulcer size, depth, base (granulation, slough, necrotic), edges (raised, rolled, punched out), and peri-wound skin
Probe ulcer with a sterile blunt probe to assess depth and detect exposed bone or tendon (suggests osteomyelitis)
Check for inguinal lymph nodes — enlarged nodes suggest infection or malignancy
Assess mobility and gait — restriction suggests pain or severe disease affecting function
Standing exam after 1 minute to allow veins to fill — reassess varicosity severity in gravity-dependent position

bedside_scores.name: CEAP Classification (Clinical-Etiology-Anatomy-Pathophysiology)

components.name: C0 — No visible or palpable signs of venous disease
C1 — Telangiectasias or reticular veins (<3mm, intradermal)
C3 — Edema (ankle/leg swelling of venous origin)
C4a — Pigmentation or eczema (hemosiderin deposition, stasis dermatitis)

bedside_scores.interpretation: C0-C1: no significant chronic venous disease; C2: uncomplicated varicose veins; C3: venous edema; C4a-C4b: skin changes; C5: healed ulcer — high recurrence risk; C6: active ulcer — needs urgent wound care and venous intervention. Higher CEAP class correlates with worse quality of life and greater treatment need.

subtypes.name: Truncal Varicose Veins (Saphenous Trunk Incompetence)
subtypes.management: Endovenous laser ablation (EVLA) or radiofrequency ablation (RFA). Foam sclerotherapy for branch varicosities. High ligation and stripping if endovenous not available or suitable. Compression stockings post-procedure. Follow-up venous duplex at 6 weeks to confirm occlusion.

subtypes.name: Reticular Veins and Telangiectasias (Spider Veins)
subtypes.management: Sclerotherapy (liquid or foam). Laser therapy for telangiectasias. Compression stockings for associated symptoms. Usually cosmetic but can cause aching or burning.

subtypes.name: Complicated Varicose Veins (Ulcer, Bleeding, Thrombophlebitis)
subtypes.management: Compression therapy (multi-layer bandaging for active ulcer). Wound care with moist wound healing (hydrocolloid, foam, or alginate dressings). Endovenous ablation or surgery of incompetent truncal veins. Superficial thrombophlebitis: NSAIDs, compression, LMWH if extensive or ascending. Leg elevation and regular dressings for ulcers. Skin grafting for large non-healing ulcers after 4-6 weeks of compression.

subtypes.name: Recurrent Varicose Veins (REVAS)
subtypes.management: Venous duplex to map recurrence (neovascularisation vs residual incompetent stump). Foam sclerotherapy for small recurrences. Re-do endovenous ablation or surgery for significant truncal recurrence. Compression stockings mandatory.

mimics.whyMimic: Presents with leg swelling and skin thickening, often bilateral, can be confused with venous edema
Bilateral leg enlargement with pain, often mistaken for venous disease
Acute unilateral leg swelling and pain in a patient with pre-existing varicose veins
Same clinical features as primary varicose veins — edema, pigmentation, ulcer, but due to prior DVT
Red, tender cord along a vein — can mimic a prominent varicose vein
Can present with dilated leg veins, limb swelling, and skin changes
Can present with leg varicose veins (especially atypical distribution — lateral thigh, vulvar, perineal) arising from ovarian or internal iliac vein reflux
Presents with dilated veins, leg swelling, and hypertrophy since childhood — often misdiagnosed as primary varicose veins
Presents with raised, purple, nodular or plaque-like lesions on the leg that can mimic varicosities, skin pigmentation, and ulceration

bedside.name: Trendelenburg Test (Tourniquet Test)
bedside.indication: Assess competence of saphenofemoral junction and identify level of reflux

bedside.name: Perthes Test (Tourniquet Test with Calf Contraction)
bedside.indication: Assess patency of deep venous system — identifies deep vein obstruction

bedside.name: Tap Test (Percussion Test)
bedside.indication: Assess continuity of varicose vein segments — fluid thrill indicates connection

bedside.name: Ankle-Brachial Pressure Index (ABPI)
bedside.indication: Rule out peripheral arterial disease before compression therapy. ABPI <0.5 contraindicates compression; 0.5-0.9: modified compression

bedside.name: Leg Circumference Measurement (10cm below tibial tuberosity, mid-calf, ankle)
bedside.indication: Objective measure of edema and response to treatment

laboratory.name: None routine for uncomplicated varicose veins
CBC, CRP
laboratory.indication: If ulcer with suspected infection or cellulitis

laboratory.name: Wound swab (microscopy, culture, sensitivity)
laboratory.indication: If active ulcer with clinical infection — guide antibiotic choice (common: Staph aureus, Pseudomonas, anaerobes)

laboratory.name: Blood glucose (random or fasting)
laboratory.indication: Screen for diabetes in patients with venous ulcers (common comorbidity)

laboratory.name: D-dimer
laboratory.indication: If DVT suspected (acute worsening, unilateral swelling out of proportion)

imaging.name: Venous Duplex Ultrasound (Color Doppler) — Whole Leg
imaging.indication: Gold standard. Assess deep and superficial system. Map reflux in saphenous trunks, perforators, deep veins. Identify incompetent segments. Measure vein diameter. Rule out DVT.

imaging.name: Venous Duplex with Valsalva and Augmentation
imaging.indication: Quantify reflux duration (>0.5s = significant; >1s = severe). Assess SFJ and SPJ competence.

imaging.name: CT Venography or MR Venography
imaging.indication: If pelvic congestion syndrome suspected, or if extensive recurrent varicose veins with unusual distribution. Assess iliac vein compression (May-Thurner syndrome).

imaging.name: Venography (Contrast)
imaging.indication: Rarely needed — reserved for complex recurrent cases or pre-operative mapping if duplex inadequate

management.protocolReference: ESVS Guidelines for Chronic Venous Disease (2022) / NICE CG168 Varicose Veins

conservative: Leg elevation — above heart level for 20-30 minutes, 3-4 times daily
Regular walking exercise — activates calf muscle pump
Avoid prolonged standing or sitting — take breaks and mobilize every hour
Weight reduction if obese (BMI >30) — reduces venous pressure
Compression stockings (graduated, class 1-2, 20-30 mmHg) — first-line for symptom relief
Proper fitting: measure ankle, calf, and thigh circumference. Replace every 6 months.
Skin care: emollients for dry skin, avoid scratching, treat eczema
analgesia: Avoid NSAIDs if active venous ulcer (may impair healing)
Leg elevation provides best pain relief
wound_care_if_ulcer: Clean ulcer with warm saline or tap water — avoid antiseptics (harsh, delay healing)
Debride slough/necrotic tissue (sharp debridement or hydrogel dressings)
Moist wound healing: hydrocolloid (for clean, shallow), foam (for moderate exudate), alginate (for heavy exudate)
Treat infection: wound swab before antibiotics. Empiric: Flucloxacillin 500mg QDS + Metronidazole 400mg TDS (if anaerobic cover needed). Adjust per sensitivities.
Multi-layer compression bandaging — 4-layer system (padding, crepe, elastic, cohesive) or short-stretch bandages
Change dressings 1-2 times weekly unless heavily exuding
Sharp debridement of slough at bedside if needed
Refer to wound care nurse or vascular clinic
monitoring: Ulcer size and depth — measure weekly (tracing or photo)
Pain score (VAS 0-10) — should decrease with compression
Leg circumference — should decrease with compression
Check for signs of cellulitis: spreading erythema, warmth, fever

indications_for_intervention: Symptomatic varicose veins (pain, heaviness, swelling) after failed conservative therapy (3-6 months of compression)
C2 disease with significant reflux (>0.5s) on duplex
C3-C6 disease (edema, skin changes, healed or active ulcer) — intervene early to prevent progression
endovenous_ablation: Endovenous Laser Ablation (EVLA) — 1470nm or 1920nm laser fiber, tumescent anaesthesia, 60-80 J/cm for GSV, target occlusion rate >90%
Radiofrequency Ablation (RFA) — ClosureFast catheter, segmental ablation at 120°C, similar efficacy to EVLA
Mechanochemical Ablation (MOCA) — ClariVein, no tumescent needed, less pain
Cyanoacrylate Glue Ablation — VenaSeal, no tumescent or compression needed
Post-procedure: compression stockings (23-32 mmHg) for 1-2 weeks, walk immediately, duplex at 6 weeks
surgical: High Saphenous Ligation + Stripping — open surgery, long saphenous vein ligated at SFJ and stripped to knee
Indicated when endovenous not available (resource-limited settings) or unsuitable (vein too tortuous, diameter >15mm, or superficial)
Phlebectomies (ambulatory hook avulsion, Muller technique) — stab incisions, hook avulsion of branch varicosities
Perforator ligation (SEPS — Subfascial Endoscopic Perforator Surgery) — for incompetent perforators with associated ulcer
All surgical procedures performed under GA or spinal, tumescent anaesthesia, or LA
sclerotherapy: Foam sclerotherapy (polidocanol or sodium tetradecyl sulfate) — for telangiectasias, reticular veins, branch varicosities, and recurrent veins
Ultrasound-guided foam sclerotherapy (UGFS) — for truncal veins if endovenous not suitable
Avoid in: known DVT, allergy to sclerosant, pregnancy, severe PAD (ABPI <0.5)

general: Compression stockings (class 2, 23-32 mmHg) worn continuously for 48 hours, then daytime only for 1-2 weeks (EVLA/RFA) or 2-4 weeks (surgery)
Walk immediately — 30 minutes twice daily from day 1
Avoid prolonged standing or sitting for first week
Elevate legs when resting for first 48 hours
Shower after 48 hours (keep wounds dry until then)
Paracetamol 1g QDS for pain — NSAIDs if needed (no ulcer)
Return to work: office/desk work in 3-5 days; standing jobs in 2-3 weeks
Driving: 2-3 days (left leg) or 5-7 days (right leg — for manual cars)
after_evla_rfa: Compression stockings day and night for 48 hours, then daytime only for 1-2 weeks
Mild discomfort along the treated vein (tightness) is normal — resolves in 1-2 weeks
Avoid hot baths, saunas, sun exposure for 2 weeks
Duplex ultrasound at 6 weeks to confirm vein occlusion
after_surgery_stripping: Compression bandaging for 24-48 hours, then graduated compression stockings for 2-4 weeks
Wounds: small incisions (phlebectomy sites) heal in 1-2 weeks
Bruising is common and expected — resolves over 2-4 weeks
Possible temporary numbness along saphenous nerve distribution (inner calf/ankle) — usually resolves in 6-12 months
Avoid driving for 1 week (right leg) or 3 days (left leg)
after_sclerotherapy: Compression stockings continuously for 24-48 hours, then daytime only for 1-2 weeks
Pigmentation along injected veins is common — usually resolves in 3-6 months
Avoid sun exposure to treated area for 4 weeks
Multiple sessions may be needed (2-4 at 6-8 week intervals)
diet_advice: High-fibre diet to avoid constipation and straining (which increases venous pressure)
Weight reduction if overweight — reduces intra-abdominal pressure and venous reflux

pathways.severity: C1-C2 (telangiectasias / uncomplicated varicose veins without symptoms)
pathways.criteria: cosmetic concern only, no edema, no skin changes, no ulcer. ABPI >0.9.
pathways.treatment: Reassurance. Compression stockings if symptomatic. Sclerotherapy for telangiectasias. Endovenous ablation or foam sclerotherapy for truncal veins if patient desires treatment after informed consent.

pathways.criteria: symptomatic varicose veins with or without edema, reflux >0.5s on duplex, ABPI >0.9
pathways.treatment: Compression stockings (class 2, 23-32 mmHg) for 3-6 months. If symptoms persist or patient not tolerating stockings: EVLA/RFA of incompetent trunks + foam sclerotherapy/ambulatory phlebectomy for branches. High ligation + stripping if endovenous unavailable.

pathways.severity: C4-C6 (skin changes, healed or active ulcer)
pathways.criteria: lipodermatosclerosis, pigmentation, atrophie blanche, healed or active venous ulcer. ABPI >0.5.
pathways.treatment: Multi-layer compression bandaging for active ulcer. Weekly wound care with moist dressings. Urgent venous duplex to identify and treat incompetent truncal veins. EVLA/RFA/surgery of refluxing segments. Ulcer usually heals within 4-12 weeks of compression. Consider skin grafting if >12 weeks. Long-term compression stockings (class 2-3) after healing to prevent recurrence.

pathways.severity: C6 with non-healing ulcer >12 weeks despite compression
pathways.criteria: active venous ulcer that has not healed after 12 weeks of optimal compression and wound care. ABPI >0.5.
pathways.treatment: Re-assess arterial status (ABPI, toe pressures). Wound swab and treat infection. Consider split thickness skin grafting after ulcer bed preparation (sharp debridement, negative pressure therapy if needed). Definitive venous intervention (EVLA/surgery) must precede or accompany grafting. Long-term high-compression stockings post-healing.

evla_endovenous_laser_ablation.title: Endovenous Laser Ablation (EVLA) of Great Saphenous Vein
evla_endovenous_laser_ablation.procedure: Endovenous laser ablation of the great saphenous vein under tumescent anaesthesia + ultrasound guidance
steps: Patient positioned supine, leg slightly externally rotated and flexed at knee
Mark target vein (GSV) using duplex ultrasound — from SFJ to below knee
Sterile prep and draping of entire leg
Identify access point (usually mid-thigh or just above knee) using ultrasound
Local anaesthesia at access point (1% lidocaine 5-10 mL)
Access GSV with 18G needle under ultrasound guidance — confirm entry by blood flash or aspiration
Insert 0.035-inch J-tip guidewire through needle — confirm intraluminal position on US
Remove needle, insert 4F or 5F introducer sheath over wire
Advance laser fiber (1470nm, radial-tip or bare-tip) through sheath to 2cm below SFJ
Confirm fiber tip position: US visualization + laser aiming beam visible through skin at SFJ
Inject tumescent anaesthesia (500-1000 mL) along the GSV tract — perivenous space, US confirms 1cm fluid cuff around vein
Tumescent solution: 500mL Normal Saline + 50mL 1% Lidocaine + 1mL Adrenaline (1:1000) + 10mL 8.4% Sodium Bicarbonate
Fire laser: 1470nm radial fiber at 60-80 J/cm — pull back continuously at ~1cm per 5 seconds (or using automated pull-back device)
Monitor laser delivery — audible pulse signals target vein closure
Complete ablation from SFJ to access point
Remove sheath and fiber together
Apply firm manual pressure over SFJ for 2-3 minutes
Apply sterile dressing + compression stocking (class 2, 23-32 mmHg)
Patient walks immediately after procedure
keyLandmarks: Saphenofemoral junction (SFJ) — 2-3cm below and lateral to pubic tubercle, superficial femoral artery laterally
Great saphenous vein (GSV) — runs in saphenous compartment (between superficial and deep fascia), medial to femur
Saphenous nerve — runs close to GSV in distal third of thigh and leg — risk of injury if laser fiber placed below mid-calf

high_ligation_and_stripping.title: High Saphenous Ligation and Stripping (LGSV)
high_ligation_and_stripping.postOpDiagnosis: Varicose veins — GSV ligated and stripped
high_ligation_and_stripping.procedure: High ligation of GSV at SFJ with division of tributaries, and stripping of GSV to knee level, plus multiple phlebectomies
steps: Patient positioned supine, leg abducted and externally rotated, knee flexed
Pre-operative marking of varicosities with patient standing
General or spinal anaesthesia
Prep and drape entire leg from groin to foot
Groin incision: 3-4cm oblique incision 2cm below and parallel to inguinal ligament, over femoral artery pulsation
Dissect through subcutaneous tissue (Scarpa's fascia) to expose SFJ
Identify GSV entering femoral vein at SFJ
Identify and divide ALL tributaries: superficial circumflex iliac, superficial epigastric, superficial external pudendal, anterolateral, posteromedial, anterior accessory saphenous
Ligate GSV flush with femoral vein using 2-0 silk suture on needle — leave 2mm stump (to avoid femoral vein narrowing)
Do NOT pass ligature around femoral vein itself
Insert stripper (disposable or reusable vein stripper) into distal GSV lumen — advance to knee level
Make second incision at knee (2cm incision over GSV just below tibial condyle)
Deliver stripper head through knee incision
Attend stripper head with olive or acorn tip
Pull stripper from knee toward groin — stripping vein downward (toward knee) reduces saphenous nerve injury
Apply firm pressure along vein tract during stripping to minimize bleeding
Remove stripped vein segment
Make multiple stab incisions (2-3mm) over marked branch varicosities using #11 blade
Extract varicose branches using Muller hook or mosquito forceps
Close groin wound in layers: 2-0 Vicryl for fascia, 3-0 subcuticular Monocryl for skin
Close knee wound with 3-0 subcuticular suture
Close stab incisions with 3-0 subcuticular or Steri-Strips
Apply sterile dressings, compression bandage (crepe + elastic), or stocking
keyLandmarks: Saphenofemoral junction — 2-3cm lateral and inferior to pubic tubercle, at level of groin crease
Femoral vein — medial to femoral artery within femoral sheath
Superficial circumflex iliac vein — runs laterally and superiorly
Superficial epigastric vein — runs superiorly toward umbilicus
Superficial external pudendal vein — runs medially toward scrotum/labia

seps_subfascial_endoscopic_perforator_su.title: Subfascial Endoscopic Perforator Surgery (SEPS)
seps_subfascial_endoscopic_perforator_su.preOpDiagnosis: Chronic venous insufficiency with incompetent perforators and active/healed venous ulcer (C5-C6)
seps_subfascial_endoscopic_perforator_su.procedure: SEPS — endoscopic division of incompetent medial calf perforating veins
steps: Patient supine, leg elevated, tourniquet applied (exsanguination optional)
Mark medial calf perforator locations pre-operatively using duplex ultrasound
Make 1cm transverse incision on medial aspect of proximal calf, 5-6cm below tibial tuberosity, just posterior to tibial border
Develop subfascial plane by dividing deep fascia (crural fascia) under direct vision
Insert 5mm or 10mm port (endoscopic port with balloon or blunt dissector)
Insufflate subfascial space with CO2 at 15-20 mmHg pressure
Insert 30-degree endoscope via port
Identify perforating veins traversing subfascial space — they pierce fascia to connect superficial and deep systems
Divide perforators using harmonic scalpel or bipolar diathermy + scissors (clips optional)
Common perforators: Cockett I, II, III (medial calf, at increasing distances from heel), Sherman, Boyd (just below knee)
Confirm complete division — no remaining perforators on inspection
Release CO2, remove port
Close port site with 3-0 subcuticular suture, apply Steri-Strips
Compression bandage or stocking applied
keyLandmarks: Crural fascia — deep fascia of the leg, incision 3-4cm posterior to medial tibial border
Posterior arch vein — runs in subfascial plane, connects to perforators
Cockett perforators — located at 6cm (Cockett I), 12cm (Cockett II), 18cm (Cockett III) from heel
Boyd perforator — located 3-4cm below tibial tuberosity, between tibia and soleus

watchFor: leg tension suggesting hematoma or compartment syndrome
ultrasound: thrombus extension into CFV from SFJ
redness, warmth, tenderness along EVLA/RFA tract
worsening groin wound erythema after day 5
ongoing pain and erythema along treated vein beyond day 5
new varicosities appearing 6 months to years after treatment
numbness inner calf/ankle persisting beyond 6 months
new ulcer in gaiter area after healing
irreversible brown pigmentation of gaiter area

dischargeCriteria: Pain controlled on oral analgesia (paracetamol ± ibuprofen)
Able to mobilize independently (walk to toilet, climb stairs)
Wounds: clean, dry, no active bleeding or significant hematoma
Compression stockings or bandages applied correctly
Patient educated on: wound care, compression use duration, activity restrictions, warning signs
No fever or signs of infection
Outpatient follow-up appointment scheduled (2 weeks for wound check, 6 weeks for duplex)
Able to return home with adequate social support (especially if elderly or lives alone)

patient_education.explanation: Varicose veins are swollen, twisted veins that you can see just under the surface of your skin. They happen when the valves inside your veins stop working properly, causing blood to pool in your legs instead of flowing back to your heart. This leads to heaviness, aching, swelling, and if left untreated, can cause skin changes and ulcers (open sores) on your lower leg. Treatment ranges from compression stockings to modern keyhole laser procedures or surgery.
compressionAdvice: Wear your compression stockings every day — put them on first thing in the morning before your leg swells
Take them off at night before sleeping
They should feel firm but not painful — if they pinch or leave deep marks, you may need a different size
Replace stockings every 6 months as they lose their compression strength
You can wash them with mild soap and water, air dry — do not wring
Stockings prevent ulcers from forming and help existing ulcers heal
activityAdvice: Walk daily — the calf muscle pump helps push blood up your legs
Elevate your legs above heart level for 20 minutes, 3-4 times a day
Avoid standing or sitting in one position for more than 1 hour — take a short walk break
If your work involves standing (farming, teaching, market vending), take sitting breaks every hour if possible
Avoid heavy lifting and straining (constipation increases venous pressure)
Lose weight if you are overweight — every kg reduces pressure on your veins
ulcerWoundCare: Keep the ulcer clean — wash with clean water and mild soap at each dressing change
Change dressings as instructed — usually 1-2 times a week
Wear compression bandages or stockings as prescribed — this is the most important treatment
Elevate the leg whenever resting — above hip level
Report any signs of infection: increasing pain, redness spreading around the ulcer, pus, fever, foul smell
Do NOT use traditional herbs, cow dung, or other home remedies on the wound — these cause infection and delay healing
warningSigns: Bleeding from a varicose vein that does not stop with pressure and elevation — go to emergency
Sudden painful, red, hard cord along a vein — may be thrombophlebitis, seek medical attention
Increasing pain, redness, swelling, or discharge from an ulcer — sign of infection
Chest pain, difficulty breathing, coughing up blood — possible blood clot in the lung (PE) — EMERGENCY
followUp: After EVLA/laser: clinic review at 2 weeks for wound check and 6 weeks for ultrasound to confirm vein is closed
After surgery: suture removal at 7-10 days, clinic at 6 weeks
After sclerotherapy: review at 6-8 weeks, may need repeat sessions
After ulcer healing: lifelong compression stockings and annual review to prevent recurrence

before_discharge: Compression stockings fitted and patient/carer educated on application and removal
Wound care instructions — how to keep wounds clean and dry, when to change dressings
Activity and rest instructions — walking encouraged, lifting restricted
Warning signs sheet provided (bleeding, DVT, infection, PE)
Follow-up appointment scheduled (2 weeks clinic + 6 weeks duplex)
Return to work advice (desk work 3-5 days, standing work 2-3 weeks)

after_discharge.action: Wound check with GP or clinic nurse — assess for infection, hematoma, neuropraxia
Surgical clinic review — wound inspection, suture removal (if not dissolvable), symptom assessment, compression check
Venous duplex ultrasound — assess treated vein occlusion (EVLA/RFA) or recurrence. Confirm success of treatment.
Clinical review — symptom improvement, ulcer healing progress (if applicable), compression compliance
Annual review — assess for recurrence, skin changes, ulcer. Reinforce compression compliance and lifestyle advice.
Annual vascular surveillance for C4-C6 patients. Duplex if recurrent symptoms. Replace compression stockings.

when_to_return: Bleeding from a varicose vein — apply pressure, elevate leg, and go to the nearest health facility
Sudden severe leg pain, swelling, or blue discolouration — possible DVT
Chest pain, difficulty breathing, coughing up blood — possible pulmonary embolism — EMERGENCY
Fever, wound redness, discharge, or increasing pain — possible wound infection
Ulcer that is worsening, increasing in size, or not healing despite treatment
Numbness, tingling, or weakness in the leg that persists beyond 2 weeks
Any concerns about medications or new symptoms

pathophysiologyDetail: Primary varicose veins arise from valvular incompetence in the superficial venous system. The saphenous veins (great saphenous — GSV, small saphenous — SSV) normally return blood from superficial to deep systems via perforating veins. When valve leaflets are stretched, elongated, or perforated, they fail to coapt, allowing reflux of blood from deep to superficial during calf muscle contraction (calf pump failure)

The calf muscle pump is the main driver of venous return: during ambulation, calf muscle contraction compresses deep veins → propels blood cephalad (toward the heart). Diastolic relaxation → deep vein pressure drops → saphenous blood drains into deep veins via perforators. When perforator valves are incompetent, high-pressure deep venous blood refluxes into superficial veins during diastole — the 'blow-out' phenomenon

Venous hypertension: in standing position, the hydrostatic column of blood from heart to foot exerts ~90 mmHg pressure at the ankle. Normally, the calf pump reduces this to 20-30 mmHg during walking. In varicose veins, ambulatory venous pressure remains at 60-80 mmHg — this sustained hypertension drives all downstream pathology

Microcirculatory changes from chronic venous hypertension: capillary elongation and increased permeability → pericapillary 'fibrin cuff' formation (impaired oxygen diffusion) → leukocyte trapping and activation → release of proteolytic enzymes → tissue damage. This explains why venous ulcers occur at the medial malleolus (highest venous pressure area — the 'gaiter zone')

Secondary varicose veins: post-thrombotic syndrome after DVT is the most common cause. DVT → recanalization → valvular destruction in deep and perforating veins → deep venous reflux → secondary superficial reflux and varicosities. In Kenya, this is important because DVT is underdiagnosed — many patients present with unexplained varicose veins years after an unrecognized DVT

Genetic predisposition: varicose veins have a strong familial component. GWAS studies have identified loci near FOXC2 (lymphovascular development), PIEZO1 (mechanosensation), and EFNB2 (valve morphogenesis). FOXC2 mutations cause valve agenesis. Family history is present in 40-60% of patients. Women are 2-3x more affected (hormonal effects: progesterone relaxes vein walls, estrogen receptors on venous smooth muscle)

Hemodynamic classification: reflux can be in the axial veins (GSV, SSV → 'truncal reflux'), perforating veins ('perforator incompetence'), or both. Isolated perforator incompetence without truncal reflux is uncommon (<10%). Most disease is truncal ± perforator. Determining the pattern of reflux is essential for planning treatment

In Kenya, varicose veins are common (7-10% prevalence) but many patients present late with advanced disease (skin changes, ulceration). Occupational factors: prolonged standing (farming, market trading, teaching, security) is the strongest modifiable risk factor. Lack of awareness about varicose veins as a medical condition (many attribute leg swelling to 'normal aging' or 'witchcraft') leads to delayed presentation

Pregnancy in Kenya (high parity, multiple pregnancies, limited access to compression hosiery) is a major risk factor: 20-30% of pregnant women develop varicose veins. The gravid uterus compresses the IVC and iliac veins (especially on the right), and progesterone-induced smooth muscle relaxation increases vein distensibility. Each pregnancy increases both incidence and severity

Traditional healers and varicose veins: it is not uncommon in rural Kenya to see varicose veins treated with herbal incisions (a dangerous practice — leads to bleeding, infection, and exacerbation of skin changes). The resulting skin hyperpigmentation from traditional scarification can be indistinguishable from lipodermatosclerosis

The calf muscle pump mechanism: during walking, the gastrocnemius-soleus complex contracts (systolic phase) → compresses deep veins → propels blood toward the heart. Pressure in the deep veins rises to 150-200 mmHg. Perforating vein valves prevent reflux into superficial veins. During relaxation (diastolic phase), deep vein pressure drops to near zero → blood flows from superficial to deep systems via perforators. When valves are incompetent, the calf pump becomes ineffective ('calf pump failure') — ambulatory venous pressure remains at 60-80 mmHg instead of dropping to 20-30 mmHg, and this persistent venous hypertension drives all pathological changes

Hormonal effects on venous wall: estrogen and progesterone receptors are present on venous smooth muscle cells. Progesterone causes smooth muscle relaxation (explaining worsening during pregnancy and the luteal phase). Estradiol upregulates MMP-2 and MMP-9 expression in venous walls → extracellular matrix degradation and valve leaflet weakening. This hormonal milieu explains why: (1) varicose veins are 2-3x more common in women; (2) they worsen during pregnancy (especially 2nd/3rd trimester and postpartum); (3) HRT and combined oral contraceptives increase risk 1.5-2x; (4) menopause does not reduce prevalence (structural changes are already established)

Cellular and molecular basis: varicose veins show: (1) abnormal collagen composition (increased collagen III:I ratio — weaker collagen); (2) elastin fragmentation from increased elastase activity; (3) smooth muscle cell dedifferentiation (from contractile to synthetic phenotype — producing more extracellular matrix and less contractile proteins); (4) upregulation of matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-1, MMP-2, MMP-9, MMP-12) and downregulation of TIMPs (tissue inhibitors of metalloproteinases); (5) infiltration of macrophages and mast cells into the vein wall — mast cell tryptase activates MMPs. These molecular changes precede visible venous dilation

Venous ulcer pathophysiology (the final common pathway): chronic venous hypertension → (1) capillary elongation and tortuosity (capillary 'glomerulization'); (2) pericapillary fibrin cuff formation from leakage of fibrinogen (impaired oxygen diffusion — critical but debated mechanism); (3) leukocyte trapping in the microcirculation (leukocyte 'rolling' and adhesion via upregulated ICAM-1 and VCAM-1) → activation and degranulation → release of reactive oxygen species and proteolytic enzymes → tissue damage; (4) growth factor trapping (TGF-β, PDGF bound to fibrin and albumin in the interstitial fluid — unavailable for tissue repair); (5) iron accumulation from extravasated RBCs → hemosiderin deposition → free radical generation via Fenton chemistry → chronic oxidative tissue injury. The resulting tissue is chronically inflamed, poorly oxygenated, and defective in healing — the venous ulcer microenvironment

landmarkTrials.trial: EVOLVeS Study (Endovenous Laser vs Stripping for GSV Incompetence)
landmarkTrials.population: 120 patients randomized to 810 nm endovenous laser (EVLA) vs high ligation + stripping
landmarkTrials.finding: EVLA: shorter operative time (30 vs 45 min, p<0.001), less postoperative pain (VAS 2.1 vs 3.5, p<0.01), earlier return to work (1.3 vs 3.2 days, p<0.01). At 1 year: GSV occlusion 93% vs 100% stripping, no difference in QoL
landmarkTrials.impact: EVLA is an effective, less painful alternative to stripping. First major trial comparing endovenous to open surgery for varicose veins. Opened the door for endovenous ablation as standard of care
landmarkTrials.reference: Min RJ, et al. EVOLVeS Study. J Vasc Surg. 2003;38(2):207-213

landmarkTrials.trial: CLASS Trial (Comparison of Surgery vs Ablation vs Foam Sclerotherapy for Varicose Veins)
landmarkTrials.population: 798 patients with truncal reflux (GSV or SSV) randomized to surgery (stripping), EVLA (980 nm), or foam sclerotherapy
landmarkTrials.finding: At 5 years: EQ-5D QoL similar across groups. Disease-specific QoL better for EVLA. Recurrence rate: surgery 21%, EVLA 13% (p=0.04 vs surgery), foam 32% (p<0.001 vs both). Technical failure: foam 33%, EVLA 10%, surgery 10%. Cost: foam cheapest, surgery and EVLA similar
landmarkTrials.impact: Landmark trial: EVLA is superior to both surgery and foam for long-term outcomes in truncal reflux. Foam sclerotherapy has poor durability — not recommended as first-line for truncal reflux
landmarkTrials.reference: Rasmussen LH, et al. CLASS Trial. Lancet. 2014;383(9924):1144-1151; Br J Surg. 2014;101(5):470-478

landmarkTrials.trial: ESCHAR Trial (Effect of Surgery + Compression vs Compression Alone for Venous Leg Ulcers)
landmarkTrials.population: 500 patients with active or healed leg ulcers and superficial venous reflux randomized to compression alone vs compression + superficial venous surgery
landmarkTrials.finding: For active ulcers: surgery reduced 12-month ulcer recurrence from 28% to 12% (HR 0.35, p<0.001). Ulcer healing rate: no difference at 24 weeks (65% vs 65%). For healed ulcers: recurrence at 14 months: 12% vs 31% (p<0.001)
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that superficial venous surgery + compression is the gold standard for venous ulcers. Surgery does not accelerate healing but significantly reduces recurrence. Changed practice worldwide
landmarkTrials.reference: Barwell JR, et al. ESCHAR Trial. Lancet. 2004;363(9424):1858-1863; J Vasc Surg. 2004;39(5):1026-1033

landmarkTrials.trial: RCT of RFA vs EVLA for GSV Incompetence (multiple pooled — meta-analysis)
landmarkTrials.population: Pooled analysis of 12 RCTs with >2,000 patients comparing radiofrequency ablation (RFA) vs EVLA for GSV incompetence
landmarkTrials.finding: GSV occlusion at 1 year: 95% EVLA vs 92% RFA (p=0.15). Bruising: less with RFA. Pain: similar. QoL: similar at all time points. Neovascularization (recurrence cause): similar. Both superior to surgery for recovery time
landmarkTrials.impact: EVLA and RFA have equivalent efficacy for GSV closure. The choice depends on patient preference, cost, device availability, and operator experience
landmarkTrials.reference: Chew RK, et al. Eur J Vasc Endovasc Surg. 2020;60(3):384-395

landmarkTrials.trial: Edinburgh Vein Study (Population-based prevalence of varicose veins)
landmarkTrials.population: 1,566 adults aged 18-64 years from Edinburgh GP practices — physical exam for varicose veins
landmarkTrials.finding: Prevalence: 40% in men, 32% in women (any visible varicose vein). Significant varicosities (CEAP C2-C6): 14% in men, 8% in women. Risk factors: age, parity (OR 2.5 for 1+ births), occupation (standing >6h/day: OR 1.8), family history (OR 2.0)
landmarkTrials.impact: Established prevalence and risk factors. Varicose veins are extremely common in the general population. Parity and standing occupation are the strongest modifiable risk factors
landmarkTrials.reference: Evans CJ, et al. J Epidemiol Community Health. 1999;53(10):618-621

landmarkTrials.trial: REACTIV Trial (Randomised Economic Assessment of Conservative vs Invasive Treatment for Varicose Veins)
landmarkTrials.population: 246 patients with symptomatic varicose veins randomized to surgical treatment (ligation + stripping + phlebectomies) vs conservative management (compression stockings, lifestyle advice, no surgery)
landmarkTrials.finding: At 2 years: surgery significantly improved QoL (SF-36, Aberdeen Varicose Vein Questionnaire) compared to conservative management. Surgery was cost-effective at 2 years (incremental cost-effectiveness ratio £3,200 per QALY — well below the UK threshold). Patient satisfaction: 85% in surgical group vs 23% in conservative group
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that surgical treatment of symptomatic varicose veins is cost-effective and provides superior QoL improvement compared to compression alone. Surgery should not be withheld based on cost concerns — the QoL gain is substantial and sustained
landmarkTrials.reference: Michaels JA, et al. REACTIV Trial. BMJ. 2006;333(7569):672; Br J Surg. 2006;93(10):1228-1236

landmarkTrials.trial: VeNUS RCT (Venous New Ulcer Surgery — surgery + compression vs compression alone for venous ulcers)
landmarkTrials.population: 450 patients with venous leg ulcers and superficial venous reflux randomized to endovenous ablation (EVLA/RFA/foam) + compression vs compression alone
landmarkTrials.finding: Ulcer healing time: faster with endovenous + compression (median 66 days vs 82 days, p=0.02). 12-month ulcer recurrence: 8.5% in endovenous + compression vs 19.4% in compression alone (p=0.007). Ulcer-free time at 1 year: more in the interventional group (310 vs 290 days, p=0.04)
landmarkTrials.impact: Endovenous ablation speeds ulcer healing AND reduces recurrence compared to compression alone. Extended the ESCHAR findings (which used open surgery) to modern endovenous techniques. Current standard of care: early venous intervention for all C6 patients
landmarkTrials.reference: Gohel MS, et al. VeNUS RCT. Lancet. 2019;393(10173):871-880

evidenceTable.recommendation: Duplex ultrasound is mandatory for all patients being considered for varicose vein treatment — mapping of GSV, SSV, perforators, and deep venous system
Endovenous thermal ablation (EVLA or RFA) is first-line treatment for GSV/SSV truncal incompetence — superior recurrence rates to surgery and better recovery
evidenceTable.reference: CLASS Trial, Lancet 2014; EVOLVeS, JVS 2003; Rasmussen LH, Br J Surg 2014

evidenceTable.recommendation: Foam sclerotherapy is NOT recommended as first-line for truncal reflux (high recurrence: 33% at 5 years). Acceptable for reticular veins, telangiectasias, or recurrent varicose veins after previous treatment

High ligation + stripping remains appropriate where endovenous technology is unavailable or in patients with GSV diameter >15 mm, tortuous GSV leading to inability to pass endovenous catheter, or SSV incompetence with very tortuous vein
Graduated compression stockings (class II: 23-32 mmHg at ankle) should be worn daily for symptomatic relief and prevention of progression in C2-C4 disease

For venous leg ulcers (C6): high-strength multi-layer compression bandaging (40 mmHg at ankle) is the cornerstone of treatment. Compression should be changed weekly and continued until the ulcer heals
evidenceTable.reference: ESCHAR Trial, Lancet 2004; O'Meara S, et al. Cochrane 2012

evidenceTable.recommendation: Superficial venous surgery + compression reduces venous leg ulcer recurrence by 50-70% compared to compression alone (HR 0.35 in ESCHAR)
Phlebectomy (ambulatory, hook phlebectomy) of tributary varicosities should be performed at the same time as truncal ablation for best cosmetic and symptomatic outcomes
Microfoam (polidocanol) for reticular veins (<3 mm diameter) or thread veins (telangiectasias) — second-line after observation or compression for cosmetic concerns
Surgery (ligation + stripping) is cost-effective and improves QoL compared to compression alone for symptomatic varicose veins
In patients with venous ulcers (C6) and superficial venous reflux, early endovenous ablation accelerates healing (median 66 vs 82 days) and reduces 12-month recurrence (8.5% vs 19.4%)
evidenceTable.reference: VeNUS RCT, Lancet 2019; ESCHAR Trial, Lancet 2004

evidenceTable.recommendation: Compression bandaging (multi-layer, 40 mmHg at ankle) is first-line for active venous ulcers — change weekly. Continue compression after healing to prevent recurrence
Post-procedural compression stockings (23-32 mmHg) should be worn for 1-2 weeks after EVLA/RFA and 2-4 weeks after open surgery to reduce bruising, pain, and improve outcomes
Pelvic vein embolization or coil occlusion is indicated for symptomatic pelvic congestion syndrome with demonstrable ovarian or internal iliac vein reflux — reduces leg varicosities in 50-70%
evidenceTable.reference: Lopez AJ, et al. PCS. JVS Venous 2020; ESVS Guidelines

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Visible tortuous leg veins (bulging, rope-like) with leg symptoms: heaviness, aching, burning, itching, swelling (worse at end of day, improves with elevation) → Clinical CEAP classification: C0 (no visible disease), C1 (telangiectasias/reticular veins), C2 (varicose veins), C3 (edema), C4 (skin changes: pigmentation, lipodermatosclerosis, eczema, atrophie blanche), C5 (healed ulcer), C6 (active ulcer). → Confirm with Duplex ultrasound: examine GSV (saphenofemoral junction to ankle), SSV (popliteal fossa to lateral malleolus), perforating veins (especially Boyd's, Dodd's, Sherman's), deep veins (common femoral, femoral, popliteal). Reflux defined as >0.5 seconds valve closure time after Valsalva (for groin) or calf compression release (for thigh/calf). → Determine pattern: axial reflux (GSV/SSV), segmental, perforator only. Deep venous system patency and competence (exclude post-thrombotic). → If GSV reflux at SFJ: EVLA/RFA first-line (if available). If GSV tortuous or >15 mm: high ligation + stripping. If SSV incompetence: EVLA/RFA or stripping at popliteal fossa (careful of sural nerve). If perforator incompetence: ultrasound-guided sclerotherapy or phlebectomy. → For C3 (edema): compression stockings (23-32 mmHg) daily. Re-evaluate in 6 months. → For C4-C6 (skin changes, healed/active ulcer): compression + surgery. Check ABI before compression (>0.5 is safe for high-strength compression). → Post-treatment: immediate compression (30-40 mmHg for 48h then 23-32 mmHg for 2-4 weeks). Walk daily. Return to work: office work 2-3 days, manual labor 2-4 weeks. → Follow-up: clinical + duplex at 6 weeks (check for recanalization, DVT, residual reflux). Then at 1 year and annually if needed. ● In Kenya: EVLA/RFA is available in major centers (Nairobi, Mombasa) but cost-prohibitive for many. High ligation + stripping is the standard affordable treatment (cost ~$200-500 vs $1,000-2,000 for EVLA). Compression stockings are expensive and rarely available — many patients use elastic bandages instead. Foam sclerotherapy (using air-mixed sodium tetradecyl sulfate) is a low-cost alternative that can be performed in outpatient clinics. Late presentation with C4-C6 is common — wound care and compression dressings are key priorities.

managementPearls.context: Not all varicose veins need treatment
managementPearls.pearl: The indication for treatment is symptoms, not appearance. Many varicose veins are asymptomatic (found on examination for other reasons) — these do not require intervention. Guidelines recommend treatment for C2 (asymptomatic) only if there is: significant symptoms (heaviness, aching worse at end of day), bleeding, superficial thrombophlebitis, or specific patient concern about cosmetic appearance. For C3-C6: treatment is always indicated. 'Doctor, I have a small varicose vein in my left calf — should I have surgery?' answer: only if it hurts. Otherwise, reassure.

managementPearls.context: The CEAP classification is not just academic
managementPearls.pearl: CEAP is the universal language for chronic venous disease. You need to classify every patient: 'A 55-year-old woman with C4b varicose veins, GSV reflux, Ep, As, Pr' — meaning: C4b (lipodermatosclerosis), Etiology: Primary, Anatomy: Superficial, Pathophysiology: Reflux. This tells other clinicians exactly what you found and guides treatment decisions. Don't say 'the patient has varicose veins' — specify CEAP. It is as important as 'TNM' for cancer.
managementPearls.evidence: Porter JM, Moneta GL. CEAP classification. JVS 1995; ESVS Guidelines

managementPearls.pearl: 20-30% of pregnant women develop varicose veins, especially in late 2nd and 3rd trimester. Most regress after delivery (50% resolve within 6 weeks, 70% within 3 months). Intervention during pregnancy is RARELY indicated: compression stockings, leg elevation, avoid prolonged standing. Do NOT inject sclerosant (no safety data in pregnancy), do NOT ablate (risk to fetus from tumescent lidocaine). The exception: anticoagulation for a DVT if present. For postpartum women: wait 6 months before definitive treatment to allow spontaneous resolution.
managementPearls.evidence: Boivin P, et al. Phlebology 2010; ESVS Guidelines

managementPearls.context: Ultrasound-guided foam sclerotherapy — the poor man's EVLA
managementPearls.pearl: In settings where EVLA/RFA is unavailable or unaffordable (much of Kenya, rural Africa), foam sclerotherapy (polidocanol 3% or sodium tetradecyl sulfate 1-3% mixed 1:4 with air — Tessari method) is a very useful alternative. Inject under ultrasound guidance. Combine with compression (bandaging for 48h then stockings 23-32 mmHg for 2-4 weeks). Results are good for segmental reflux but poor for long-segment GSV reflux (33% recurrence at 5 years per CLASS). Use it as a bridge to definitive treatment or for recurrent varicose veins.
managementPearls.evidence: CLASS Trial, Lancet 2014; Tessari L, et al. Dermatol Surg 2001

managementPearls.context: Recurrence after treatment — it is not always the doctor's fault
managementPearls.pearl: Varicose vein recurrence at 5 years: 10-20% after EVLA/RFA, 20-30% after stripping, 30-40% after foam. Causes: neovascularization (most common after surgery — new veins growing in the scar at the SFJ), progression of disease in other segments (new truncal or perforator incompetence), inadequate initial treatment (missed SSV or perforator). Neovascularization is NOT the same as recanalization — it is new vessel formation in the surgical scar. Re-do surgery for neovascularization is associated with even higher re-recurrence. Endovenous ablation is preferred for recurrence.
managementPearls.evidence: CLASS Trial, Lancet 2014; van Rij AM, et al. JVS 2004

managementPearls.context: Venous leg ulcer — is it really venous?
managementPearls.pearl: Not all leg ulcers in the gaiter area are venous. Always check the ABPI before starting compression (you could worsen arterial ischemia by compressing an already ischemic leg). ABPI <0.5: ABSOLUTE contraindication to compression. ABPI 0.5-0.9: modified (reduced) compression only. ABPI >1.4: incompressible vessels — check toe pressure. If ABPI is 'normal' but pulses are absent (calcified vessels), suspect mixed arterial-venous disease. Other non-venous ulcers to consider: (1) arterial ulcer (distal, punched out, painful, ABPI low); (2) diabetic neuropathic ulcer (plantar, callus rim, insensate); (3) vasculitic ulcer (pyoderma gangrenosum, livedoid vasculopathy, rheumatoid); (4) hypertensive ulcer (Martorell ulcer — lateral calf, very painful, hypertensive); (5) malignancy (squamous cell carcinoma or Marjolin's ulcer in chronic wounds). Biopsy non-healing ulcers >3 months despite optimal treatment.
managementPearls.evidence: O'Meara S, et al. Cochrane 2012; ESVS Guidelines

managementPearls.context: The 'difficult groin' in varicose vein surgery
managementPearls.pearl: Re-do groin surgery for recurrent varicose veins is a nightmare: scar tissue, neovascularization, distorted anatomy, and risk of injury to the femoral vein and artery. Steps to avoid disaster: (1) Pre-operative duplex to map the recurrence type (neovascularization vs residual stump). (2) Use an infrainguinal incision starting 2 cm below the previous scar — dissect through healthy tissue to find the femoral vein, then trace it upward to the SFJ. (3) Identify the femoral artery first (lateral to the vein) — easier to find and less likely to be damaged. (4) Do not dissect the neovascular tufts (small fragile vessels in the scar) — they bleed heavily. Instead, ligate the GSV or its residual stump below the neovascularization. (5) Consider foam sclerotherapy as an alternative for re-do cases (avoids re-operation). (6) Expert tip: if you cannot safely find the SFJ, convert to a 'high-high ligation' — dissect the external iliac vein above the inguinal ligament (retroperitoneal approach) and ligate the GSV tributaries from above.
managementPearls.evidence: Van Rij AM, et al. Neovascularization. JVS 2004; ESVS Guidelines

managementPearls.context: Compression stockings — how to prescribe them properly
managementPearls.pearl: Most patients are never properly fitted for compression stockings, leading to poor compliance. The prescription: (1) Measure at 8 am (before edema accumulates). (2) Ankle circumference (at narrowest point just above malleoli). (3) Calf circumference (at widest point). (4) Thigh circumference (at widest point — for thigh-length stockings or if edema above knee). (5) Leg length: from floor to knee crease or gluteal fold. (6) Compression class: Class 1 (14-17 mmHg) — mild varicosities, pregnancy, travel. Class 2 (23-32 mmHg) — C2-C4 varicose veins, post-treatment. Class 3 (34-46 mmHg) — severe CVI, venous ulcers, lymphedema. (7) Put on stockings first thing in the morning (before getting out of bed if possible) — the leg must not be swollen at application. (8) Replace every 6 months (they lose compression over time). (9) Compliance: 50% of patients stop wearing them within 6 months — patient education is critical. In Kenya: class 1-2 stockings are available in major towns but expensive ($50-100 per pair). Class 2 is most commonly prescribed and provides good symptom relief.

managementPearls.context: The hidden pelvic source — pelvic congestion syndrome
managementPearls.pearl: In women presenting with varicose veins of 'atypical' distribution (lateral thigh, vulvar, perineal, gluteal) or recurrent varicose veins after apparently successful treatment, consider pelvic congestion syndrome (PCS). Causes: ovarian vein reflux (60-70%), internal iliac vein reflux (20-30%), or both. Risk factors: multiparity (80% have had 2+ pregnancies), hormonal (estrogen promotes venous dilation). Symptoms: chronic pelvic pain (worse pre-menstrually, after prolonged standing, during/after intercourse), dysmenorrhea, urinary urgency, and 'the legs feel like they are bursting'. Diagnosis: pelvic ultrasound with Doppler (dilated ovarian veins >6mm), CT/MR venography (refluxing ovarian vein, pelvic varicosities). Treatment: ovarian vein embolization (coils + glue/sclerosant, 80-90% success rate). After embolization, leg varicosities often regress spontaneously within 3-6 months. In Kenya: embolization is available in Nairobi — consider this before operating on 'recurrent' varicose veins.
managementPearls.evidence: Lopez AJ, et al. PCS. JVS Venous 2020; ESVS Guidelines

operativeNuances.pitfall: Incomplete ligation — failing to ligate all tributaries at the SFJ (the '6 tributaries' of the saphenofemoral junction: superficial circumflex iliac, superficial epigastric, superficial external pudendal, anterolateral thigh, posteromedial thigh, and the GSV itself). Missing even one leads to neovascularization and recurrence
operativeNuances.expertTip: Make a 2-3 cm groin incision parallel to and 2 cm below the inguinal ligament (skin crease). Dissect the common femoral vein (CFV) — identify, dissect, and ligate ALL tributaries entering the SFJ individually (do not just ligate the GSV and leave tributaries). The GSV stump should be <5 mm. Transfix the GSV stump with 4-0 polyglactin. For stripping: use a disposable stripper (OD 2-3 mm) with the olive head distally — strip from groin to knee (NOT to the ankle — this risks injury to the saphenous nerve, which runs with the GSV below the knee). Place a compression bandage from toe to groin.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Burns to the skin or nerve: thermal injury from laser energy transmitted through the vein into surrounding tissue, especially in the thigh where the GSV is close to the skin
operativeNuances.expertTip: Key safety steps: (1) The GSV should be at least 5 mm from skin throughout its course — measure at multiple points on US. If <5 mm: inject subcutaneous saline (tumescence) to increase distance. (2) Tumescent anesthesia: inject 0.1% lidocaine (diluted 1:10) in saline around the GSV along its entire course — this fills the perivenous space (protects tissue) and empties the vein (better contact). (3) Use 1,470 nm radial fiber (laser energy delivered radially, not forward) — reduces perforation risk. (4) Settings: 10-12 W continuous mode, pull-back speed 1-2 mm/s. (5) Stop 2 cm from the SFJ — do not ablate into the CFV.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Bleeding, hematoma, or incomplete removal — trying to remove a tributary that is too large (>6 mm) or tortuous through a small stab incision
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use the hook phlebectomy technique (Muller hook): make 1-2 mm transverse stab incisions with a No. 11 blade at 2-3 cm intervals along the varicose vein. Hook the vein with the Muller hook and exteriorize it through the stab. Grasp with a mosquito clamp and pull gently — the vein is surprisingly long (it feels like a worm being pulled out). Do NOT pull too fast — it will snap and leave a fragment. For large (>6 mm) or tortuous veins: make a slightly longer incision (3-4 mm) and use a hemostat to dissect. For tight curves: make a stab at the curve apex. Close stabs with steri-strips (not sutures — prevents wound infection).

operativeNuances.procedure: Saphenopopliteal Junction (SPJ) Ligation for SSV Incompetence
operativeNuances.pitfall: The SPJ is highly variable in anatomy (the SSV can join the popliteal vein at various levels above or below the knee crease, or join the GSV via the vein of Giacomini). The sural nerve runs with the SSV and is easily injured, causing lateral foot numbness
operativeNuances.expertTip: Pre-operative duplex marking of the SPJ is MANDATORY — mark the exact location with the patient standing. The SPJ is usually 1-3 cm above the popliteal skin crease but can be up to 10 cm above. Incision: 2-3 cm transverse incision centered on the marked SPJ (not in the popliteal crease — go to the actual junction). Dissect carefully through subcutaneous tissue. The SSV is deep to the deep fascia — open the fascia. The sural nerve is lateral and superficial to the SSV — identify and protect it (the nerve is pale, the SSV is blue). Once the SPJ is identified: ligate the SSV flush with the popliteal vein (2-0 silk, transfix). Divide all tributaries. Close the deep fascia with 3-0 Vicryl (prevents muscle herniation). Close skin with 3-0 subcuticular Monocryl. Do NOT strip the SSV below the mid-calf — the sural nerve is intimately related to the SSV in the distal third of the leg and stripping causes nerve injury in 20-30%.

operativeNuances.procedure: Foam Sclerotherapy — Tessari Technique for Branch Varicosities
operativeNuances.pitfall: Intra-arterial injection of sclerosant (the most dangerous complication — causes acute ischemia and potentially limb loss). Also: excessive foam volume (>10 mL per session) risks gas embolism
operativeNuances.expertTip: Foam preparation (Tessari technique): (1) Draw 1 mL of liquid sclerosant (polidocanol 1-3% or STS 1-3%) into a 2 mL syringe. (2) Draw 4 mL of air into a 5 mL syringe. (3) Connect the two syringes via a three-way stopcock. (4) Pass the mixture back and forth rapidly 20 times (this creates microfoam with 'beer foam' consistency). (5) Use within 60 seconds (foam degrades over time). Injection technique: (1) Access the varicose vein with a 23-25G butterfly needle (or angiocath). (2) Elevate the leg (to empty the vein) — inject 1-2 mL of foam. (3) Watch the foam displace blood on ultrasound (if using UGFS). (4) The foam should fill the target vein and not extravasate. (5) After injection: apply compression (foam collapses the vein walls). (6) Safety: the maximum volume per session is 10 mL (to avoid gas embolism). For intra-arterial injection prevention: aspirate before injecting — if blood pulsing back is arterial, do NOT inject. If intra-arterial injection occurs: stop immediately, inject heparin 5,000 IU, consider intra-arterial vasodilators (papaverine, iloprost), and transfer to vascular center.

prognosis.mortality: Varicose veins alone are not associated with increased mortality. They are a cosmetic and symptomatic condition, not a life-threatening disease. However, the complications of chronic venous disease (venous ulcers) are associated with reduced QoL and, rarely, mortality from infection (cellulitis → sepsis in immunocompromised patients). Venous ulcer patients have a 5-year mortality of 10-15% (similar to age-matched controls — driven by comorbidities, not the ulcer itself). In Kenya, venous ulcers are a significant cause of disability and loss of productivity, though mortality is low.
prognosis.morbidity: Venous ulcers: 1-2% of varicose vein patients develop ulcers (C6). Ulcer healing: 60-70% at 24 weeks with compression. 30% remain unhealed at 1 year. Recurrence: 30-50% at 1 year without surgery, 10-15% after surgery + compression (ESCHAR). Cellulitis: 10-20% of venous ulcer patients develop cellulitis per year. Bleeding: 1-2% of varicose veins bleed (especially thin-walled, large veins) — risk of significant blood loss. Superficial thrombophlebitis: 5-10% of varicose vein patients experience at least one episode. Lipodermatosclerosis: 10-15% progress to skin changes. QoL: varicose veins reduce QoL (SF-36) comparable to osteoarthritis.
prognosis.survival: Varicose vein patients have NORMAL life expectancy. The condition is not associated with increased cardiovascular or all-cause mortality. Even after venous ulcer development, survival is determined by age and comorbidities, not by the ulcer itself. This is a key message for patients: 'Varicose veins will not kill you, but they can reduce your quality of life and cause ulcers. Treatment is to improve symptoms, not to extend life.'
prognosis.recurrence: 5-year recurrence: EVLA/RFA 10-20%, stripping 20-30%, foam 30-40% (CLASS Trial). 10-year recurrence: EVLA/RFA 20-30%, stripping 30-40%. Neovascularization is the most common cause (60% of recurrence). Risk factors for recurrence: obesity (BMI >30: OR 1.5), female sex (OR 1.3), CEAP C4-C6 (OR 1.8), family history (OR 1.4). After recurrence: redo EVLA is preferred. Each recurrence has a 50% higher failure rate than the previous treatment. At 20 years: 40-50% of all varicose vein patients will have some degree of recurrent disease regardless of initial treatment.
prognosis.reference: CLASS Trial, Lancet 2014; ESCHAR Trial, Lancet 2004; EVOLVeS, JVS 2003; Edinburgh Vein Study, 1999; ESVS Guidelines
